title: [cm/revision-lock] setNewRevision

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'hard': [revision-lock] Setting `new_revision` assumes a centrally serialized revision/update state, which cannot prevent concurrent writers from producing conflicting revisions under eventual consistency without coordination or a resolution rule.

title: [source] Only adjustments attached to logs whose status is exactly "done" and whose times

given: An asset has a done increment of 5 timestamped after the current/requested cutoff.
It also has a pending increment of 3 timestamped before the cutoff.
It has a done increment of 2 before the cutoff.
when: Inventory is calculated at that cutoff.
then: Only the done pre-cutoff increment contributes, so the result includes 2 but not 5 or 3.

why_non_obvious: A materializer could naturally apply every recorded adjustment, or regard future-dated done records as current; both are explicitly excluded.

source_read.rule: Only adjustments attached to logs whose status is exactly "done" and whose timestamp is at or before the requested cutoff contribute; a null cutoff means request time, not an unbounded history.

title: [source] A quantity’s observable label is always its entity ID; the separate stored `labe

given: A quantity has ID `42`.
Its stored `label` field is `Harvest weight`.
when: The quantity label is read for a link, list, or display.
then: The returned label is `42`, not `Harvest weight`.

why_non_obvious: The entity declares `label` as its label key and also has a user-facing Label field, so a conventional port would naturally display that field instead of the numeric/assigned ID.

source_read.rule: A quantity’s observable label is always its entity ID; the separate stored `label` field does not determine `label()` output.

title: [source] A reset is an ordered assignment, not an additive adjustment: calculation starts

given: An asset has increment +10 at timestamp 100.
It has reset 4 at timestamp 200.
It has decrement 1 at timestamp 300.
when: Inventory is calculated after timestamp 300.
then: The result is 3: the +10 before the latest reset timestamp is excluded, reset assigns 4, and the later decrement is applied.

why_non_obvious: The adjustment type might be modeled as a normal event in an additive ledger, and selecting only one latest reset event would miss same-timestamp ordering behavior.

source_read.rule: A reset is an ordered assignment, not an additive adjustment: calculation starts at the most recent reset timestamp and processes records in ascending (timestamp, log ID) order, with each reset replacing the running total.

title: [source] A newly created, unsaved asset returns no inventory summaries at all, rather tha

given: An asset has not yet been persisted and is marked new.
when: Inventory is requested for the asset.
then: The result is an empty array.

why_non_obvious: A read model may initialize an inventory projection for every asset with zero values, but this API distinguishes a new asset from an existing asset with no adjustments.

source_read.rule: A newly created, unsaved asset returns no inventory summaries at all, rather than zero-valued summaries.

title: [source] Events sharing the latest reset timestamp are retained and sequenced by ascendin

given: At timestamp 200, log ID 10 increments an asset by 3.
At timestamp 200, log ID 11 resets it to 4.
At timestamp 200, log ID 12 decrements it by 1.
when: Inventory is calculated after timestamp 200.
then: All three same-timestamp events are processed by ID; the result is 3, because +3 is overwritten by reset 4 and then -1 is applied.

why_non_obvious: The reset cutoff is timestamp-inclusive rather than a strict event-position cutoff, so timestamp alone does not define the reset boundary.

source_read.rule: Events sharing the latest reset timestamp are retained and sequenced by ascending log ID; therefore an adjustment before that reset at the same timestamp is overwritten, while one after it is applied.

title: [source] The migration `create_quantity` transform creates and persists one quantity per

given: The migration process receives a scalar source value such as `12`.
when: `create_quantity` transforms that value.
then: It returns an empty array and creates no quantity.

why_non_obvious: The plugin advertises `handle_multiples`, and its name suggests it can create a quantity from any source value, but the implementation silently does nothing for non-iterable input.

source_read.rule: The migration `create_quantity` transform creates and persists one quantity per element only when its input is an array or Traversable; scalar input produces an empty result.

title: [source] Inventory is calculated independently per exact (measure, units) pair; adjustmen

given: An asset has a mass increment in kilograms.
The same asset has a mass increment in pounds.
when: Inventory is requested for the asset with no units filter.
then: Two separate inventory summaries are returned, one per units ID; their values are not summed or converted.

why_non_obvious: A caller may treat an omitted filter or a measure-only request as a cross-unit total, but the implementation discovers distinct pairs and calculates each separately.

source_read.rule: An empty measure or units argument means the null/unset category during calculation, not a wildcard; however the public inventory read with only one filter omitted enumerates the distinct pairs found for the supplied filter.

title: [source] For each migrated quantity, configured static defaults are applied first, config

given: `default_values` supplies `measure: count`.
`values` maps `measure` from source property `measure`.
An input element has `measure: weight`, while the parent row has `measure: length`.
when: The element is transformed.
then: The created quantity receives `weight`; if validation reports any violation, a skip-row exception is raised and it is not saved.

why_non_obvious: A straightforward implementation might use only parent-row values, let defaults win, or save first and report validation later; this plugin has a specific precedence order and fail/skip behavior.

source_read.rule: For each migrated quantity, configured static defaults are applied first, configured mapped values overwrite them, each mapped source property prefers the individual element’s property over the parent row’s property, and a validation violation skips the migration row instead of saving an invalid quantity.

title: [source] Quantity values are revisioned snapshots: measure, fractional value, units, stor

given: A quantity belongs to a quantity type with its default `new_revision` setting.
The quantity is edited, changing its value or measure.
when: The edit is saved.
then: The updated fields belong to a new revision rather than erasing the prior revision snapshot.

why_non_obvious: A quantity looks like a small value object, and the revision UI is explicitly hidden, so an implementation could model edits as ordinary in-place updates despite revision state being retained.

source_read.rule: The permitted measure codes are a fixed, closed catalog (including nonphysical measures such as `value`, `rate`, `rating`, `ratio`, and `probability`), rather than being inferred from the selected unit.

title: [source] The `plain_text` quantity view mode is rendered first and then normalized by dec

given: A rendered quantity in `plain_text` mode contains `A&B`, markup, newlines, and repeated spaces.
when: The view is rendered.
then: The output is tag-free, contains `A&B`, has no leading/trailing whitespace, and has each whitespace run replaced by one space.

why_non_obvious: “Plain text” could be implemented as raw field text or merely HTML stripping; the actual output additionally decodes entities and normalizes all newlines/tabs/spaces after full rendering.

source_read.rule: The `plain_text` quantity view mode is rendered first and then normalized by decoding HTML entities, stripping tags, trimming edges, and collapsing every whitespace run to one ordinary space.

title: [cm/revision-lock] QuantityType

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] The `new_revision` setting assumes edits produce revision history, but revision histories can converge after synchronization and transiently differing revision views are tolerable; therefore this is not inherently a coordination-required lock invariant.

title: [cm/revision-lock] Quantity

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] The `'revision' => 'revision_id'` mapping indicates revision tracking rather than an enforced single-writer lock, so revision history can converge eventually and transient concurrent revisions are tolerable under eventual consistency.

title: [cm/revision-lock] baseFieldDefinitions

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] The source assumes the entity is revisionable via `setRevisionable(TRUE)`, and eventual consistency can preserve and materialize concurrent revision history eventually even if revisions briefly coexist without a global lock.

title: [domain-logic] QuantityInterface

given: the domain state this class operates on
when: its primary operation is exercised
then: the value it computes / maintains is observed at the boundary

why_non_obvious: A high-reach domain class concentrates behavior many members depend on; its non-obvious rules are load-bearing for a faithful port.

title: [field-computed] AssetInventoryItemList

given: the underlying state the computed field derives from
when: that state changes
then: the computed field reflects the change without a separate write

why_non_obvious: A computed field list derives its value from other state rather than storing it. A port that stores it as a plain field diverges when the source state changes.

title: [hook-implementation] ThemeHooks

given: the entity state the hook keys on
when: the lifecycle event that triggers the hook occurs
then: the hook's derived side effect is observable at the value boundary

why_non_obvious: A hook fires a side effect on an entity lifecycle event. A re-implementer who models only the primary write can miss the derived state the hook maintains.

title: [views-filter] QuantityViewsData

given: records that the filter would include and exclude
when: the filtered read is taken
then: only the included records are returned, in the filter's order

why_non_obvious: A Views query/filter shapes what a read returns (visibility, ordering, exclusion). The filter semantics are invisible from the read's method name.
